coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots from Indian Bael patra as effective sensing tool to detect perilous food colorant
coredata.dc:creator: Vijeata, Anjali
Chaudhary, Savita
Chaudhary, Ganga Ram
coredata.dc:description: Herein a simple strategy has been demonstrated for the synthesis of environmentally amiable and highly fluorescent carbon dots from the most useful plant of Indian classical Ayurveda i.e. Bael patra fruit. The morphological features and chemical composition of the prepared carbon dots were characterized through High resolution transmission electron microscopy, Field emission scanning electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Owing to their highly emission nature, the applicability of carbon dots was tested against various food colorant i.e. Allura red. Under the optimized conditions, the decreased fluorescence intensity exhibited a good linear relationship with increasing concentration of Allura red. Additionally, an extensive research was carried out to determine the adsorption efficiency of carbon dots for Allura red and heavy metals. Based on the context, here we report the novelty of this work, demonstrating the decontamination of various samples from Allura red and heavy metals with the application of carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bael Patra
Food colorant
Carbon dots
Allura red
Metal ions
Amino acids

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of carbon dots from Bael Patra fruit extract
Adsorption of dye on carbon dots surface
Optimization of optical response of CDs at different pH
Optical response of pure C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs towards amino acids and metal ions
Detection of Allura red via fluorescence quenching of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs
Application of carbon dots in real food/water samples
Optical response of CDs towards amino acids
Reusability of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for the synthesis of CDs by hydrothermal carbonization of different parts of Bael patra fruit.

(ai-aiii) PL emission spectrum, (bi-biii) Typical HRTEM images, (ci) UV–vis. absorption spectra and (cii) FTIR spectra of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs. Fast scan XPS (di-fi) and high resolution spectra of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs for C1S (dii-fii), O1S (diii-fiii) and N1S (div-fiv).

Concentration dependent changes in (ai-aiii) PL emission spectrum and (bi-biii) UV–vis absorption spectra of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs respectively. (c) Schematic representation of mechanism involved in the adsorption process.

Stern volmer plot of Allura red sensing at different concentrations in (a) C-CDs, (b) P-CDs and (c) M-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Relative intensity plot of AR-CDs complex in presence of metal ions, (b) Percent removal effieciency of CDs for Allura red and Fe3+ in different samples along with the (c) plausible mechanism involved. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Relative intensity plot determining the selectivity of CDs towards Allura red and Fe3+in the presence of various amino acids. (where 1 is blank, 2- Alalnine, 3- Arginine, 4-Asparagine, 5- Aspartic acid, 6- cysteine, 7- Glutamic acid, 8- Glutamine, 9- Glycine, 10- Histidine, 11- isoleucine, 12- Leucine, 13- lysine, 14- Methionine, 15- PhenylAlanine, 16- Proline, 17- Serine, 18- Threonine, 19- Tryptonine, 20- Tyrosine and 21 Valine). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots from Indian Bael patra as effective sensing tool to detect perilous food colorant

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein a simple strategy has been demonstrated for the synthesis of environmentally amiable and highly fluorescent carbon dots from the most useful plant of Indian classical Ayurveda i.e. Bael patra fruit. The morphological features and chemical composition of the prepared carbon dots were characterized through High resolution transmission electron microscopy, Field emission scanning electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Owing to their highly emission nature, the applicability of carbon dots was tested against various food colorant i.e. Allura red. Under the optimized conditions, the decreased fluorescence intensity exhibited a good linear relationship with increasing concentration of Allura red. Additionally, an extensive research was carried out to determine the adsorption efficiency of carbon dots for Allura red and heavy metals. Based on the context, here we report the novelty of this work, demonstrating the decontamination of various samples from Allura red and heavy metals with the application of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Food visual quality is one of the increasingly important and essential aspect for food manufacturers, consumers and retailers (Amchova, Kotolova & Ruda-Kucerova, 2015). In order to meet consumer demand and expectations, synthetic food colorants have been widely used in food and drinks to increase their appearance and to compensate for colour loss during food storage and processing. One of the most commonly used food colorants is Allura red which is found in certain food products such as bakery products, candies, ice cream and soft drinks (Deroco, Medeiros, Rocha-Filho & Fatibello-Filho, 2018). It is considered as synthetic azo dye as its chromophore properties are based on presence of azo group which is linked with the auxochrome hydroxy and methoxy group through aromatic π-bonded rings (Rovina, Siddiquee, & Shaarani, 2016). Also, the presence of sulphonic group in synthetic dyes provides high solubility in water. In contrast, the azo groups present in food colorants are carcinogenic in nature and can cause hyperactivity in sensitive children (Esmaeili, Ashrafi-Kooshk, Khaledian, Adibi, Rouhani & Khodarahmi, 2016). Moreover, their direct discharge could lead to water pollution since there is no adequate process capable for removal of dyes from waste water. Recent investigations have approved that the toxicological properties of food additives are highly dangerous for human health. However, most studies speculated that Allura red may cause life threatening disease such as respiratory and digestive problems as well as migraine headaches when used extensively. In this context, Allura red has been classified as illegal food colorant in United States, Denmark, Belgium, France and India. Due to the persistent use of unauthorised dyes in food stuffs, their determination in different food sources such as ice creams, bakery products, sauces have received great attention (Silva, Wong & Fatibello-Filho, 2020; Botelho, Dantas, & Sena, 2017). Consequently, various instrumental techniques that determine Allura red in food products have been established such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), solid phase extraction (SPE), electrochemical sensor, capillary electrophoresis, Thin layer chromatography (TLC) etc (Minioti et al., 2007; Asfaram et al., 2018a; Coloma et al., 2021; Dil et al., 2017; Asfaram et al., 2018b). These methods have shortcomings of tedious measurement process, time consuming and requires sophisticated equipment. Furthermore, none of the method could simultaneously determine the presence of dye as food additives both quantitatively and qualitatively along with the reproducibility of same results. To determine the authenticity of food products, spectrophotometric analysis like fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy has attracted considerable attention in the past few years.

With the advent of nanotechnology, researchers have intensively investigated the use of carbon-based materials in sensing of cationic and anionic dyes, heavy metals etc because of the synergic effects shown by these materials (Mehrabi et al., 2017; Bogireddy et al., 2020). Interestingly, it has become one of the most widely studied topics of this era. From the emission properties, solubility and excellent biocompatibility, CDs have become an entrancing nanostructured carbon material in different fields (Cai et al., 2021). However, for most of the carbon-based sensors, the selectivity limits for only one analyte (Ng, Lim & Leo, 2021; Hu & Gao, 2020). Nevertheless a few studies had utilized carbon-based materials for selective detection of two types of analytes (Li et al., 2017). For instance, Thulasi et al. (2020) have developed fluorescent CDs from vehicle exhaust soot which served as dual sensor for both tartrazine and metal ion. Similarly, Khan et al. (2019) demonstrated use of date syrup derived graphenic sand hybrid in selective detection of dye and heavy metals for water decontamination.

Bael Patra tree (Aegle Marmelos) is native to Indian subcontinent and found throughout the Indian peninsula and Southeast Asia. It belongs to Rutaceae family and indicates the presence of underground water. The main advantages of Bale patra plant derived CDs including superior biocompatibility; highly fluorescent nature with high range of surface area to volume ratio which further helped in adsorption of Allura red molecules over the surface of formed CDs has further enhanced the scope of prepared particles. This article unveils the universal application of hydrothermal carbonization for facile synthesis of Bael Patra fruit derived CDs without the need of any chemical additives. The current study investigated execution of CDs against selective detection of Allura red via spectrophotometric method and demonstrated the favourable dye adsorption mechanism, thereby revealing the powerful adsorption sites obscured in the CDs (Bakheet & Zhu 2017). The quenching effect of Allura red on the fluorescence properties of CDs was also evaluated using stern–volmer equation. Batch experiments proclaimed that C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs exhibits extraordinary potential for both Allura red and Heavy metals i.e. Fe3+ and Cu2+. Our experimental findings based on interference of amino acids speculate the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs towards Allura red and heavy metals. More importantly the CDs act a good sensor in the PL sensing of both analytes in real food commodities and the results presented good precision and no fluctuation in repeatability test. (Hu, Gao & Luo 2021; Yao et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019). The obtained experimental output validated CDs as novel scavenger for both Allura red and heavy metals.

Bael Patra fruit was collected from botanical garden, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. Allura red with analytical standard was procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Erythrosine Extra bluish, Congo Red, Trypan blue, Nile Red, Amaranth, Sudan I, Bromophenol blue were purchased from Sigma Aldrich with 99% purity. Hydrogen chloride, sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were provided by Fisher Scientific with 99.9 % purity. All the l-amino acid i.e. cysteine, lysine, glutamic acid, asparagine, valine, aspartic acid, tryptophan, serine, proline, isoleucine, methionine, phenyl alanine, arginine, histidine, alanine, tyrosine, glycine, leucine, glutamine and threonine of HPLC grade (98 % purity) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionised water obtained from ultrapure deionised, 18.2 MΩ cm−1 from cascada bio water filtration unit (Pall Corporation Ann Arbour, Michigan, USA) was used throughout the experiments. Chloride salt of different cations Ag2+, Ba2+, Bi2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Ni+ and Pb2+ were procured from Sigma-Aldrich with ≥99 % purity and used directly without any further purification. Fruit juice, cough syrup and tablets were purchased from local market of Sector 14, Chandigarh, India

The size and morphology of CDs were analysed with high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM Hitachi H-7500 electron microscope) at 100 KV. The optical properties of CDs were examined by LABINDIA UV–visible (UV–vis.) spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800 nm using quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length. The emission properties of three different CDs were acquired from Perkin Elmer LS 55 PL Spectrophotometer. To determine the purity and elemental composition of produced CDs, FE-SEM instrument (JEOLJSM 7500F) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy at 20 KeV was utilized. Fourier transform infrared spectrum was obtained using Perkin Elmer (RXI) FTIR spectrometer instrument in the wave range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. IKA C-MAG HS7 instrument was used for heating and stirring process. XPS (Physical Electronics, Model – PHI 5000 Versa Probe III) was used to detect chemical composition of prepared carbon dots. Ultracentrifugation of the water dispersed CDs were carried out on the REMI R-24 centrifuge. The power sonic 404 sonicator was used to disperse the amino acids in CDs solution. 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor (autoclave) was used for carrying out hydrothermal synthesis. The surface state for BET adsorption analysis was determined through a Microtrac BEL instrument. Zeta potential of the particles was analyzed using Delsa™ Nano C, Beckman Coulter at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon dots from Bael Patra fruit extract
ce:section.ce:para: Bael Patra belongs to Rutaceae family and is considered as indigenous fruits of India (Veer & Singh, 2019). The peel of the fruit consists of a very hard shell with green to brownish colour which depends on the ripening stage. The yellowish color pulp appears like boiled pumpkin and possess characteristic floral. The pulp also encloses small traces of gum within its few parts which has slightly sticky texture. In this study, full color fluorescent CDs were produced from fresh and healthy fruit of Bael Patra by a facile and green synthetic approach. A schematic representation for the formation of CDs via hydrothermal carbonization has been provided in Fig. 1 . The temperature used for fabrication of CDs was well optimized to obtain highly dispersed and uniform CDs.

Typically, three different components of Bael Patra fruit i.e. peel (hard shell) (C-CDs), edible pulp (P-CDs) and mixture of pulp and gum (enclosed within it) (M-CDs) were kept differently in a ceramic crucible and heated in oven at 150 °C for 10 hrs. Pre-heating the sample tends to remove moisture content from the different fruit parts. Then 2 g of each pre-treated fruit part was rinsed with water, followed by drying in oven at 150 °C for 2 hrs. After the reaction, the black coloured residue of each component was dispersed in water and stirred for 60 min. The stirring allows the dispersion of particles into water to carry out hydrothermal process. The reaction mixture was then transferred to Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave followed by heating at 180 °C for 12 hrs. After completion of the reaction, the obtained residue was separated by centrifugation and rinsed with dichloromethane to remove the unreacted organic moieties. The final solutions were washed with double distilled water and further dried at 50 °C on hot plate (Prasannan & Imae, 2013; Alexpandi et al., 2020). To obtain the purest form of CDs, the particles were further dialyzed using a 2.5 kDa dialysis membrane for 2 days. The obtained particles were utilized for further studies.

The quantum efficiency of CDs was estimated by using rhodamine B with fluorescence quantum yield of 0.63 as standard reference. The estimation of quantum yield of the prepared CDs was calculated by taking the integrated PL emission peak (excited at 330, 350, 370 nm of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs respectively) and absorbance values (at 320, 321, 350 nm for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs respectively) of chosen CDs with respect to the Rhodamine B. The aforementioned relation is given by equation:
(1)
ϕ X
=
ϕ R
I X
I R
η X 2
η R 2
A R
A X
where ϕX and ϕR refers to the quantum yield of prepared sample and reference material. The absorbance of the sample and reference is represented by AX and AR. ɳx and ɳR corresponds to the refractive index of solution and reference material. IR and IX demonstrate the fluorescence emission intensity of reference material and solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of dye on carbon dots surface
ce:section.ce:para: For adsorption experiment, C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs (5 mg) were finely crushed and added to 5 mL dye solution (3 μM to 30 μM) in a 15 mL closed glass vial. The suspension was then shaken for 24 h on a magnetic stirrer at room temperature. A blank test was always performed with each experiment which act as a reference to evaluate the amount of dye present. After 24 h, the solution was taken off from the stirrer to carry out centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 15 min to remove the solid particles and the supernatant was used for further analysis. For each dye solution, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopic analysis was performed at room temperature. The spectra were recorded at their respective maximum emission wavelength and each spectrum was the average of three-time successive scanning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of optical response of CDs at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH was accessed from comparison of absorption and emission peaks obtained at varied pH conditions (1.0–13.0). Fig. S1(a-c) shows slight bathochromic shift in the UV–vis absorption spectra with increase in pH values. The fluorescence intensity is usually responsive to different pH conditions and shifts progressively towards larger wavelength from pH 1.0 to pH 13.0 (Fig. S1(d-f)). Moreover, there was slight enhancement in the fluorescence intensity as the pH moves towards neutral conditions. Considering the experimental data, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimum pH value for carrying out further experimental study (Fig. S1(g-h). The pH variations can administer the protonation and deprotonation of surface related emissive sites which result in decrease in fluorescence intensity at relatively lower and higher pH values (Prathumsuwan, Jaiyong, In, & Paoprasert, 2019).

In order to check the stability of formed particles, the optical response of prepared carbon dots was also evaluated as function of time and in presence of saline medium with different concentration of NaCl (0–1.0 mol L-1). The stability of CDs was examined through UV light (365 nm) irradiation as a function of exposure time (0 min to 72 h) (Section S1, supplementary data). The experiment was repeated thrice and the average results were presented in Fig. S2(ai-aiii). The effect of saline medium on the emission intensity of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs were evaluated by adding different amount of NaCl in the sample. The three different carbon dots were excited at their maximum emission intensity wavelength and the corresponding effect of salinity was measured through fluorescence spectrophotometer Fig. S2(bi-biii).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response of pure C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs towards amino acids and metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction behavior of distinct metal ions such as Ag2+, Ba2+, Bi2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Ni+ and Pb2+ and different l-amino acids i.e. aspartic acid, asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine were accessed on all the three types of CDs. Prior to the fluorometric analysis, 1 mL solution of each CDs and metal ions/amino acids were incubated for 3 min and the fluorescence intensity was measured at their maximum emission wavelength (Fig. S9). The whole experiment was repeated three successive times and the outcomes were given in Fig. S9.

CDs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal carbonization process using Bael Patra fruit extract as precursor. In this study we fabricated a set of three CDs from Bael Patra fruit using its peel, pulp and gum muddled with pulp. The quantum efficiency of developed CDs was found to be 59.39 %, 57.07 % and 55.25 % for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs respectively. The UV–vis. absorption spectrum of CDs shows two peaks centred at 230 and 350 nm in case of C-CDs, 232 and 320 nm in P-CDs and 222 and 321 nm in M-CDs as shown in Fig. 2 (ci). The former peaks can be assigned to the π-π* transition of CC bond in sp2 hybridisation whereas the later is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO and CN bond (Sun, Liu, Wang, Yang, Yang & Zhuo 2020). The emission profile of developed CDs was investigated through fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs under different excitation wavelength was measured as shown in Fig. 2(ai-aiii). The obtained spectra demonstrated that the maximum emission peak was due to the excitation at 330, 350 and 370 nm in case of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs respectively (Boruah et al., 2020). The as synthesised CDs poses excellent stability on UV irradiation and in saline medium (Fig. S2). There was no significant reduction in the emission intensity of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs on UV irradiation even after three days of continuous irradiation (Fig. S2). Moreover, the presence of saline solution poses no considerable effect on the fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs (Fig S2). This negligible effect on the emission spectra of CDs over a broad range of saline conditions demonstrates that the particles were highly stable and can tolerate high amount of salinity.

To investigate the surface state of CDs, morphological and structural characterization of purified CDs were performed. The successful synthesis of CDs via hydrothermal carbonization approach was further confirmed by HRTEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) imaging of CDs, as displayed in Fig. 2(bi-biii). The HRTEM image of the CDs indicates the crystalline nature of graphitic carbon, showing the lattice fringes i.e. the spacing between two planes. The estimated interlayer distance was found to be 0.185 nm, 0.299 nm and 0.349 nm in case of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs (Fig. S3). The particle size distribution demonstrates narrow distribution of particles with average diameter of 3 nm for C-CDs, 6 nm for P-CDs and 8 nm for M-CDs. The obtained results indicate that as-prepared CDs were almost spherical in shape and monodispersed in nature (Fig. 2(bi-biii) (inset)). Therefore, the optical response of the CDs could be well explained with reference to their size calculation via HRTEM analysis. The excitation and emission spectrum of CDs depends upon the size of the particle i.e. as the particle size of the CDs gets smaller, both excitation and emission spectra of CDs shifts towards shorter wavelength. The combination of HRTEM and fluorescence results revealed comparable results for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs as given in Table S1. These results demonstrated that the quantum size effect controls the emission properties of CDs because as the particle size decreases quantum confinement takes place which further quantize energy into discrete energy levels i.e. conduction and valence band. The surface area, pore volume and pore diameter of the synthesized C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs were calculated using BET measurements (Table S2). The BET results of C-CDs showed higher surface area value as compared to other two types of formed particles. Along with that the C-CDs has also possessed highest micropore volume, whereas the M-CDs shows slightly lower surface area and comparatively less existence of micropores. The existence of these micropores over the CDs surface provides free surface-active sites for adsorption of toxins from waste water samples. However, P-CDs display lowest BET surface area and minimum number of pores over its surface in contrast to C-CDs and M-CDs. This is because the C-CDs were prepared from hard woody shell of Aegle marmelos fruit which on carbonization resulted in more carbon content and micropores over its surface. Whereas P-CDs and M-CDs prepared from pulp and mixture of gum and pulp, which gets burned quickly on carbonization and results in distortion of carbon structure along with decrease of surface area and micropores. Additionally, these results were clearly verified from FESEM images of P-CDs and M-CDs which show the presence of mesopores which further allows the diffusion of ions inside the pores.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) technique has been used to determine the various functional groups present over the surface of CDs (Fig. 2(cii)). The FTIR spectrum determined for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs revealed the sharp band for P-CDs and M-CDs at 635 cm−1 while broad band was observed in case of C-CDs. This band corresponds to = CH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1097 and 1350 cm−1 was related to distinctive absorption peaks for CO and NH stretching vibrations of CDs respectively. The peak in the higher energy region 3300 cm−1 was attributed to –OH stretching vibrations on the surface of CDs and the peak at 2950 cm−1 was allocated to –CH stretching vibration. The presence of –OH group plays a significant role in adsorption of dyes and metal ions in as-prepared CDs (Sahoo et al., 2020). In order to verify the size and crystal morphology, the as-prepared CDs were further scrutinized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) technique (Fig. S4(ai-aiii)). The particles were found to be uniform and spherical with well-defined boundaries arranged in the form of chain. The particles were interlinked with Vander Waal forces of attraction. From the corresponding elemental mapping images (Fig S4(bi-biii)) it was revealed that the CDs consist of elements like carbon (C), oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) (Myint et al., 2018). The elucidation of chemical structure of CDs was further confirmed by XPS spectroscopy. XPS can accurately determine the various functional groups present on the surface of C-CDs, P-CDs, and M-CDs through inner shell electron excitation (Fig. 2(d-f). XPS survey spectrum demonstrates that C-CDs, P-CDs, and M-CDs were mainly composed of C, O, N. The spectrum showed intense peaks for C1S and O1S and comparatively weaker peaks for N1S in case of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs (Fig. 2(di-fi)). In the high-resolution spectrum of C-CDs, C1S exhibited signals at 284.10, 285.56, 286.53, 288.26 eV which corroborated to CC, CN, CO/COC and CO groups respectively (Fig. 2(dii)). High resolution peaks at 531.72, 532.11, 533.16, 534.55 eV corresponding to CO, CO, C–OH and OC–O was given by O1S. N1S spectra show two main typical peaks at 400.47 and 401.58 eV which indicated the presence of CN and NH groups(Fig. 2(div)). The deconvoluted XPS spectra for P-CDs displayed the existence of multiple functional groups on the surface state of CDs (Fig. 2(ei)). The high-resolution spectra for C1S confirms five different surroundings at 282.61, 285.36, 286, 288.63, 289.55 eV for CC, CN, CO/COC, CO and COOH groups respectively (Fig. 2(eii)). The deconvoluted XPS spectra for O1S displays peaks at 531.97, 532.63, 533.22, 534.63 and 536.25 eV which corresponds to CO, CO, C–OH, OC–O and COC groups. Fig. 2(eiv) represents peaks at 399 and 400 eV corresponding to CN and NH functionalities obtained on deconvolution of N1S spectra. The high-resolution spectra of C1S in M-CDs exhibited binding energy peaks at 284.76, 285.2, 286.06, 288.2 eV indicating the presence of CC, CN, CO/COC and CO groups respectively (Fig. 2(fii)). Deconvoluted spectra of O1S presented peaks at 531.63, 532.25, 533.52 and 534.11 eV analogous to CO, CO, C–OH and OC–O moieties (Fig. 2(fiii)). The signal at 400.6 and 401.11 eV were attributed to CN and NH groups in the high-resolution spectra of N1S (Fig. 2(fiv)) (Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2021).

The XPS measurements determines the variations in the intensities of distinct functional groups in C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs which further indicates different structural composition of the three CDs. This technique confirms the difference in relative ratios of functional moieties consisting of C, N, O groups due to different starting precursor materials.

Before analysing the interaction of CDs with Allura red, it is necessary to understand the absorption spectrum of Allura red separately. Hence, the UV–vis. absorption measurements have been performed to understand the ground and excited state properties of Allura red. The absorption spectra of Allura red show a weak peak at 313 nm and a sharp peak at 502 nm as shown in Fig. S5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Allura red via fluorescence quenching of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: To get a better interpretation of results obtained from fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of Allura red, it is prerequisite to unravel the adsorption mechanism involved between the CDs and Allura red (Fig. 3(c)). The surface morphology of the CDs plays a key role in exposing the substantial adsorption sites present over their surface. The aforementioned results obtained from FTIR and XPS revealed the super-hydrophilic nature of CDs due to availability of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. These functional groups render the possibility of van der Waal forces of attraction such as hydrogen bonding or hydrophobic interaction with the Allura red molecules (Wu et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2000). Furthermore, it was estimated that the CDs obtained from plant sources at higher temperature conditions involves intramolecular/intermolecular interactions which leads to the carbonization, polymerization and aromatization. These versatile interactions further escort the formation of sp2 hybridised carbon, which gave rise to π-π bonding between CDs and Allura red (Fig. 3c). Additionally, there could be the possibility of electrostatic interactions which results from reaction between positively charged NH3 + groups of CDs and SO3 - group of Allura red. N2 adsorption–desorption analysis further supported the higher adsorption site over the surface of formed particles for the adsorption of Allura red. In addition, the zeta potential values of pure CDs also supported the electrostatic interaction among Allura red and formed CDs. The negative value of zeta potential for AR-CDs complex further confirmed the effective adsorption of dye over the surface of prepared CDs.

The persuasive mechanism designed for decreased value of PL intensity was also substantiated through static or dynamic quenching behaviour in formed particles (Damera et al., 2020). Theoretically, both these mechanisms were believed to play significant role in monitoring the interaction involved in AR-CDs complex. In general, the dynamic quenching occurs due to collision whereas static quenching results from ground state complex formation between photoluminescent material and quencher. The static quenching constant decreases with increase in temperature whereas the dynamic quenching constant values increases with increase in temperature. Therefore, to investigate the whole mechanism, the emission spectra were measured at two different temperatures (298 K, 308 K) for the C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs (Fig. 4). From the results, it was observed that the larger amount of fluorescence quenching was observed at higher temperature which resulted from the diffusion of Allura red molecules inside the pores of CDs.

The degree of interaction between Allura red and CDs were further calculated through Stern Volmer equation (Farmani, Peyman & Roshanfekr, 2020) i.e.
(2) Fo/Fn = 1 + Ksv [Q]
where Fo and Fn indicates the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher, Q determines the concentration of Allura red. From equation (2), Ksv value was calculated for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs at 298 K and 308 K. The results showed significant increase in Ksv values due to appreciable interaction between molecules at higher temperature, thereby giving rise to dynamic quenching. The respective values of slope and intercept at 298 K and 308 K were given in Table S4. Table S5 shows the various analytical parameters calculated for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs at different temperatures. The LOD was calculated at 298 K and 308 K using the standard 3σ/ slope and values were given in Table S6. The LOD values exhibits a decrease with increase in temperature from 298 K to 308 K. Hence the results obtained from above proposed mechanism (Fig. 3c) were found to be in good agreement with dynamic quenching.

In general, the probability of occurrence of heavy metals along with food colorants in the market products is quite higher nowadays. Owing to their pernicious effect on human health as well as environment, it is essential to study the detection of heavy metals along with Allura red. To understand the complexation tendency of metal ions with AR-CDs complex, fluorescence analysis was executed in the presence of different metal ions (Ag2+, Ba2+, Bi2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Ni+ and Pb2+). Before analyzing the effect of metal ions on the AR-CDs complex, the corresponding effect of metal ions on pure CDs has been determined (Fig. S9d). The presence of negatively charged COO–, –OH groups checked using XPS and FTIR studies supported the adsorption of metal ion over the surface of formed CDs. Interestingly, the results show that only a few metals ions like Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni+ show higher affinity towards CDs whereas a large range of metals do not show any considerable effect on the fluorescence intensity of pure CDs. This might be explained due to the electrostatic interactions with hydroxyl and carboxylic groups over the surface of CDs.

However, the binding affinity between AR-CDs complex and metal ions gets enhanced in contrast to pure CDs (Fig. 5 a). This may be attributed to the increased electrostatic interactions of metal ions in presence of both Allura red and CDs molecules. Also, the negative zeta potential values of AR-CDs complex i.e. AR-C-CDs (-0.11), AR-P-CDs (-0.05) and AR-M-CDs (-0.09) further enhanced the binding potential of AR-CDs with metal ions (Fig. 5a). Interestingly, distinct proportion of fluorescence quenching was observed with different metal ions at same concentration. It was observed that most metal ions showed little or no effect on the fluorescence intensity of AR-CDs complex, thereby indicating less binding potential of metal ions towards AR-CDs complex. However, Cu2+ and Fe3+ quenched the PL spectra of CDs to greatest extent followed by Co2+, Pb2+, Ni+ (Fig. 5a). Among both of them, Fe3+ quenched the spectra dramatically which was due to its higher affinity towards phenolic hydroxyl groups over the surface of CDs which further increased the non-radiative recombination of excitons. On the other hand, quenching in case of Cu2+ result from high affinity and fast chelation of Cu2+ with nitrogen and oxygen present on the surface of CDs (Fig. 5c). Hence, from the above outcomes, it can be inferred that the adsorption of heavy metals is mainly due to ion chelating negatively charged functional groups concealed over the CDs surface. Furthermore, the adsorption tendency of most metal ions was minimum in case of P-CDs, which concludes that P-CDs possess less surface energy adsorption sites in contrast to C-CDs and M-CDs. The distinct emission pattern generated by each metal ion in AR complex of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs is presented in Fig. S12.

To further investigate the quenching behaviour of Fe3+, the fluorescence spectra of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs at different concentration of Fe3+ were recorded. The Allura red concentration was kept constant viz 15 μM throughout the sensitivity studies. The fluorescence intensity exhibits a gradual decrease with increasing concentration of Fe3+ i.e. from 0 to 150 μM. The sensitivity experiment of Fe3+ shows markedly good results with increasing concentration (Fig. S13 ai-aiii). The stern volmer plots obtained, shows a linear relationship between normalised intensity and concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. S13 bi-biii). The LOD values calculated for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs were found to be 0.145, 0.142 and 0.165 respectively. Fig. S14a shows the interference ability of other metals on the fluorescence intensity of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs. From the results, it was evident that none of the metal ions affected the CDs selectivity for Fe3+. As a result of these observations, it can be concluded that the three different CDs act as an excellent sensor for detection of Fe3+ in presence of various other metal ions. Table S9 summarizes the performance of various reported sensors in the selective detection of Allura red and heavy metals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon dots in real food/water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effectiveness of CDs, its co-adsorption properties were analysed in multi component system which contains a mixture of organic dyes and heavy metals. Since the probability of organic dyes and heavy metals is quite high in different food and water samples. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to check the applicability of system in these samples. For the analysis, the real samples were spiked with known concentration of Allura red (15 μM) and Fe3+ (50 μM) and fluorescence spectrum was recorded. From the results, it was evident that the CDs possess excellent adsorption efficiency for Allura red and Fe3+ even in the different mediums (Fig. 5b). This behavioural variation is due the presence of various functional moieties over the surface of CDs (as confirmed by XPS and FTIR results) which resulted in co-adsorption potential of CDs for both Fe3+ and Allura red. The percent recovery efficacy of both the analytes were estimated by using the following equation:
Recovery percentage = [Intensity of analyte in medium/ Intensity of analyte in water] × 100

On interpreting the results, it was found that the percent recovery efficacy for both the analytes was very high ranging from 89.41 % to 96.83 % in all chosen food and water samples (Table S8). Hence, the outcomes supported the findings that the prepared CDs were highly selective for Allura red and Fe3+ even in different real sample mediums. Though, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was also shown a quenching behaviour in presence of Fe3+ ions without Allura red. Therefore to interpret the function of the Allure red in application of real sample has also been required to perform for clearly visualizing the role of dye molecules.This was done by performing the recovery studies of CDs with spiking the real food samples with Allura red only (absence of Fe3+ ion) in current work. Fig S15 shows the calibration plots obtained in case of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs with different concentration of Allura red (3, 9, 15, 24, 30 μM) in real food samples. Table S7 shows the recovery percentage values vary between 79.07% and 102.77% for all the spiked samples with Allura red. The obtained results show the higher removal efficiency for Allura red in case of spiked samples. Therefore, the corresponding effect of Allura red cannot ignored in application of real samples. The overall difference in quenching behaviour of CDs in presence of real food smaples with both Allura red and Fe3+ ions was mainly aroused due to the binding ability with the functional moieties over the exterior facade of CDs. On the other hand, this current study clearly demonstrates the negligible effect of surrounding matrix on the sensing potential of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs for both Allura red and Fe3+. Thus, the potentiality of the developed sensor for both the analytes turns out to be an effective criterion for sensing of pollutants in contaminated water and food samples. Hence, the developed sensor works really good in presence of heavy metals ions along with the presence of perilous food colorant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response of CDs towards amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: To appraise the selectivity of CDs for Allura red and Fe3+, the adsorption experiment was investigated in the presence of different amino acids (Fig. 6 ). The optical response of pure C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs was also provided in Fig. S9(a-c). From the results, it was evident that all the chosen 21 types of different amino acids have caused negligible fluorescence alterations in presence of relatively lower concentrations of Allura red and Fe3+. However, the concentration of chosen amino acids was much higher than the allowed amount in tablets, syrups and juices. Hence it can be concluded that the developed adsorbents i.e. C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs possesses high selectivity towards Allura red and Fe3+ even in the presence of interfering amino acids. To avoid the error, the above experiment has been performed thrice and the average results were presented in Fig. 6. On interpreting the results for three types of chosen CDs, it has been observed that the maximum range of variations in fluorescence intensity was observed in case of C-CDs as compared to other chosen particles. These outcomes were mainly explained due to more surface-active sites over the surface of C-CDs as compared to P-CDs and M-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the reusability of as prepared three types of carbon dots, all of them were washed with ethanol twice followed with water after each successive run. The reusability of C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs was measured through their efficiency of adsorption for Allura red. The outcome suggests that the obtained CDs revealed excellent reusability even after 10 times of recycling (Fig. S14 b). The experimental results show that the obtained CDs act as a highly promising adsorbents for deep purification of wastewater which is impossible through various conventional techniques.

In summary, a simple, facile and low-cost approach has been reported for successful synthesis of emissive CDs from Bael Patra fruit via hydrothermal carbonization process. The quantum efficiency of developed CDs was found to be 17.39 %, 59.07 % and 55.25 % for C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs respectively. The surface morphology and functionalization of CDs was estimated with FESEM, HRTEM and XPS results. Without any surface passivation, the prepared CDs have been used for detection of Allura red through spectrophotometric method. Among the three different types of CDs, C-CDs turned out to be excellent fluorescence turn-off sensor for detection of Allura red because higher ratio of surface-active adsorption sites. The quenching efficiency obtained in case of C-CDs, P-CDs, and M-CDs were 94.10 %, 80.54 %, and 86.53 % respectively. A proposed mechanism for the adsorption of Allura red over the CDs surface is also highlighted with various types of interactions verified using zeta potential and BET studies. Furthermore, the applicability of CDs as co-adsorbate for both Allura red and heavy metals were also assessed. The limit of detection (LOD) obtained for Allura red and Fe3+ was found to be 0.607, 0.260. 0.166 and 0.144, 0.142, 0.164 respectively in C-CDs, P-CDs and M-CDs. The effect of concentration and temperature on LOD has further enhanced the applicability of prepared particles in different fields. Consequently, all the three types of CDs conferred excellent results even in the presence of distinct amino acids, food and water samples. The obtained experimental output validated CDs as novel scavenger for both Allura red and heavy metals. Our discovery provided a new insight into the use of highly fluorescent CDs in sensing food colorant and their potential future application in wastewater treatment.

Anjali Vijeata: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Savita Chaudhary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration. Ganga Ram Chaudhary: Resources, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476], DST Chandigarh and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Ganga Ram Chaudhary would like to acknowledge the support of UGC, India under the INDO-US 21st Century knowledge Initiative project [F.No. 194-2/2016 (IC)]. The authors are thankful to the Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF, CIL), Panjab University, Chandigarh. Authors are thankful to Panjab University SAIF CIL for technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131492.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
